questions.question: Why is Kafka partitioned by driver_id rather than by geographic zone for the driver location stream?
options: Partitioning by driver_id guarantees that all events from one driver arrive in order to a single consumer, preventing position jumps from out-of-order events.
Partitioning by geographic zone reduces the number of partitions needed, lowering Kafka operational cost.
Partitioning by driver_id allows Kafka to deduplicate events from the same driver automatically.
Driver IDs are shorter than zone IDs, making partition key hashing faster.
questions.explanation: Location processing requires per-driver ordering to detect cell transitions and compute velocity correctly. Partitioning by zone would mix events from many drivers into one partition, losing per-driver order guarantees.

questions.question: A driver's Redis location key has a 30-second TTL. The driver goes offline. What happens to dispatch 35 seconds later?
options: The Redis key has expired; the dispatch service gets a cache miss on the driver's position and marks the driver UNREACHABLE, excluding them from match candidates.
Dispatch continues to use the last known position until the driver explicitly sends an offline signal.
The expired key causes a Redis cluster crash because dispatch polls it every second.
The driver appears as AVAILABLE in dispatch indefinitely until a background cleanup job runs hourly.
questions.explanation: TTL-based expiry is the self-cleaning mechanism. No manual deletion or heartbeat protocol is needed — the absence of the Redis key IS the offline signal.

questions.question: A location event has AccuracyMeters = 80. What should the location worker do?
options: Drop the event — accuracy above 50m is too imprecise for dispatch matching and could assign a driver to the wrong H3 cell.
Accept the event but store it in a low-confidence bucket in Redis for use only as a fallback.
Accept the event and increase the k-ring search radius from 1 to 3 to compensate for reduced precision.
Accept the event and flag it for the fraud detection pipeline.
questions.explanation: The 50m accuracy threshold ensures H3 cell assignments are reliable. An 80m accuracy reading could place a driver in an adjacent cell, causing incorrect dispatch or surge zone assignments.

questions.question: A location event arrives with a timestamp 8 seconds older than the server's current time. What does the staleness filter do and why?
options: Drops the event — events older than 5 seconds may represent a reconnection replay of stale data, causing position jumps backward in time if accepted.
Accepts the event but adjusts the timestamp to the server arrival time before writing to Redis.
Accepts the event because network latency can legitimately cause 8-second delays on mobile networks.
Drops the event and blocks all future events from that driver for 30 seconds.
questions.explanation: Device-clock-stamped events older than 5s typically indicate buffered replay after a reconnection. Accepting them would write an old position over a newer one, causing the driver to appear to move backward.

questions.question: A driver sends two consecutive location events: one at lat/lon A, then one at lat/lon B, with a 2-second gap. The implied speed is 450 km/h. What does the location worker do?
options: Drops the second event as a teleportation detection hit — 450 km/h exceeds the 300 km/h maximum vehicle speed threshold and indicates a GPS glitch or spoofing attempt.
Accepts both events and flags the driver for manual review after accepting.
Drops both events and removes the driver from Redis pending human investigation.
Accepts the second event and overwrites the Redis position because newer events always win.
questions.explanation: The velocity filter protects against GPS jumps and spoofing. The first event is valid; only the second is suspicious. Dropping the suspicious event preserves the last known good position.

questions.question: Why does the location worker batch 100ms of events before flushing to Redis rather than writing each event immediately?
options: Batching 200 events per 100ms window reduces Redis round-trips by ~100×, dramatically improving throughput at the cost of at most 100ms additional staleness.
Redis cannot accept more than one write per second per key, so batching is required to avoid rate limiting.
Batching allows the worker to deduplicate events from the same driver within the 100ms window, halving storage costs.
Redis pipeline writes are only supported for batch sizes of exactly 100 events due to protocol constraints.
questions.explanation: Each Redis write has network round-trip overhead. Batching turns 200 individual writes into one pipeline call. The 100ms staleness budget is acceptable since the dispatch system tolerates <1s position age.

questions.question: Why is Cassandra used for historical location trails instead of PostgreSQL?
options: Cassandra's wide-row model (driver_id partition key, timestamp clustering key) makes per-driver time range queries efficient at scale, while PostgreSQL would require B-tree index scans over billions of rows.
Cassandra supports geospatial queries natively via the GEORADIUS command, unlike PostgreSQL.
PostgreSQL does not support TTL-based row expiry, making 90-day retention impossible without a cron job.
Cassandra is cheaper to run on AWS than PostgreSQL for write-heavy workloads.
questions.explanation: The access pattern is always 'give me all events for driver D between time T1 and T2'. Cassandra's partition-then-cluster model makes this a single-partition range scan. At billions of rows, this is far more efficient than a secondary index on a relational table.

questions.question: How does Uber achieve O(1) geofence containment checks for airport zones using H3?
options: The airport polygon is pre-converted to a set of H3 cells via polyfill; containment becomes a Redis SISMEMBER lookup — O(1) — rather than a polygon intersection computation.
H3 cells have built-in airport flags set by Uber's map team; a single bit check determines containment.
Uber stores geofences as bounding boxes; containment is a lat/lon range comparison, which is O(1).
Airport zones are always exactly one H3 res-9 cell, so containment is a single CellID equality check.
questions.explanation: H3 polyfill converts any polygon into a set of covering cells. Once materialized in a Redis set, containment is a constant-time SISMEMBER call rather than a point-in-polygon computation that scales with polygon complexity.
